items.q: Does 'organic' food mean the same as 'healthy' food?
items.correct: No; organic refers to how food is produced, not its nutritional value.
items.misconception: Yes; organic food is automatically more nutritious.
items.random_wrong: Organic means the food contains no calories.
items.why: Consumers assume an organic label guarantees better nutrition, when it only concerns production methods.

items.q: What does a food intolerance such as lactose intolerance involve?
items.correct: Difficulty digesting a food component, causing discomfort but not an immune reaction.
items.misconception: A life-threatening immune response like a severe allergy.
items.random_wrong: A simple dislike of the taste of the food.
items.why: Students confuse intolerance with allergy, mixing up digestive problems with immune reactions.

items.q: Why do religious beliefs affect food choice?
items.correct: Religions set rules about which foods are permitted or forbidden, such as halal or kosher.
items.misconception: Religious people simply prefer certain flavours.
items.random_wrong: Religion sets the retail price of food.
items.why: Learners reduce religious dietary rules to personal preference rather than binding cultural requirements.

items.q: A consumer with coeliac disease must choose foods that are free from:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Gluten. “Lactose” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Choosing Fairtrade-labelled coffee is mainly an example of food choice influenced by:
items.why: It's Ethical concerns. “A food allergy” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Halal and kosher rules are examples of food choice shaped by:
items.why: Correct answer: Religion. “Cost” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The appearance, smell and texture of a food affect choice through its:
items.why: It's Sensory appeal. “Price” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
